schools.summary: The law against removing the neighbour's landmark: 'thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee to possess it' — the boundary — 'thou shalt not remove thy neighbour's landmark, which they of old time have set in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee': the boundary marks set by the fathers were not to be moved, to defraud a neighbour of his land (the witnesses noting the significance — the protection of property and inheritance, the landmark the sacred boundary of each man's portion, the inheritance given by God, the bounds fixed by the fathers, not to be secretly moved for gain, the lesson — the sin of covetousness and fraud that would steal a neighbour's land by moving his boundary, the removing of the landmark a hidden theft, condemned as a crime, 'remove not the old landmark; and enter not into the fields of the fatherless,' Proverbs 23:10, 'cursed be he that removeth his neighbour's landmark,' Deuteronomy 27:17, the truth that the rights and property of the neighbour are sacred, the bounds of his inheritance to be respected, the figure — the broader principle of justice and contentment with one's own, against the covetousness that grasps at another's, 'thou shalt not covet thy neighbour's house,' Exodus 20:17, the truth that the inheritance God assigns is to be held inviolable, each man secure in his own, the figure of the respect for the rights of others, the boundaries of justice not to be moved, the warning against the greed that defrauds the weak by stealth, the landmark a witness of right, the figure of the sanctity of property and the boundaries of justice); the boundary marks of the fathers not to be moved, the hidden fraud condemned (the theft of land, Proverbs 23:10) — the law (the landmark); 'in thine inheritance, which thou shalt inherit in the land that the LORD thy God giveth thee to possess it' (the inheritance given by God, each man's portion to be held inviolable against covetousness, the sanctity of property, Exodus 20:17) — the figure (the boundaries of justice, contentment with one's own, the providence, the law, the law of witnesses to follow, vv.15) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the law against removing the landmark establish?

sides.position: The sanctity of property and the rights of the neighbour - the landmark the sacred boundary of each man's God-given portion, its removal a hidden theft and covetousness condemned (Proverbs 23:10; Deuteronomy 27:17), the inheritance God assigns inviolable against the greed that grasps at another's (Exodus 20:17)

schools.summary: The law that no charge stands on one witness, but on the mouth of two or three: 'one witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity, or for any sin, in any sin that he sinneth: at the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall the matter be established' — the rule of evidence — 'one witness shall not rise up against a man for any iniquity... at the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall the matter be established': no charge was to be sustained on a single witness, but established by two or three (the witnesses noting the significance — the safeguard against false accusation, that no man be condemned on one testimony, the requirement of two or three witnesses to establish a matter, the protection of the innocent from a single, possibly false, accuser, the lesson — the care of the law for justice and the protection of the accused, the single witness insufficient, the corroboration of two or three required, the fact established only by sufficient testimony, the truth that this rule of evidence runs through Scripture, taken up by Christ and the apostles, 'in the mouth of two or three witnesses shall every word be established,' Matthew 18:16; 2 Corinthians 13:1, 'at the mouth of two witnesses, or three witnesses, shall he that is worthy of death be put to death,' Deuteronomy 17:6, the figure — the principle of justice that demands sufficient evidence, a safeguard against slander and false witness, the truth that even an accusation against an elder requires two or three witnesses, 'against an elder receive not an accusation, but before two or three witnesses,' 1 Timothy 5:19, the figure of the order and fairness of God's justice, no condemnation without proof, the rule that guards both the innocent from false charge and the truth from a single biased voice, the figure of the rule of evidence, the matter established by two or three witnesses); one witness insufficient to condemn, the protection of the accused from a single accuser (the safeguard, 1 Timothy 5:19) — the rule (one witness insufficient); 'at the mouth of two witnesses, or at the mouth of three witnesses, shall the matter be established' (the matter established by two or three witnesses, the rule taken up in the New Testament, the corroboration required, Matthew 18:16; 2 Corinthians 13:1) — the figure (the order and fairness of God's justice, the providence, the rule, the false witness to follow, vv.16-21) — Keil, Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the requirement of two or three witnesses establish?

sides.position: The rule of evidence and the safeguard of justice - no charge sustained on a single witness, the fact established only by sufficient corroboration, protecting the innocent from false accusation; a rule taken up by Christ and the apostles (Matthew 18:16; 2 Corinthians 13:1) and binding even in an accusation against an elder (1 Timothy 5:19)

the false witness made to suffer the very penalty he sought for his brother, his malice recoiling on himself
the evil purged from Israel (Psalm 7:16)

schools.summary: That the rest would hear and fear and do no more such evil: 'and those which remain shall hear, and fear, and shall henceforth commit no more any such evil among you' — the judgment would deter others from bearing false witness (the and those which remain shall hear, and fear, the fear produced, the and shall henceforth commit no more any such evil among you, the deterrence, the figure, the public judgment a warning, that others fear and shun false witness, the truth that the punishment of the slanderer deters others from the same evil, the figure of the deterrent effect of justice, the people restrained by the example, 'others also may fear,' 1 Timothy 5:20, the fear that guards against false witness, the example a restraint, the judgment that keeps the people from such evil, vv.19); the fear produced by the judgment (the deterrence, 1 Timothy 5:20) — the fear (the rest); 'shall henceforth commit no more any such evil among you' (the people kept from such evil, the restraint, 1 Timothy 5:20, the figure, the deterrent effect of justice, the providence, the deterrence, the law of just retribution to follow, vv.21) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And those which remain shall hear, and fear, and shall henceforth commit no more any such evil among you
that the rest would hear and fear and do no more such evil
the judgment of the false witness making others fear and shun the same evil
the example a restraint (1 Timothy 5:20)

schools.summary: The law of just retribution, life for life and eye for eye: 'and thine eye shall not pity; but life shall go for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot' — the measured justice — 'thine eye shall not pity; but life shall go for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot': justice was to be exact, the penalty matching the crime (the witnesses noting the significance — the principle of just and proportionate retribution, the punishment to fit the crime exactly, neither more nor less, life for life, the measure of the law's justice, the lesson — that the law required strict and impartial justice, the penalty equal to the injury, the rule given to the judges, not to private revenge, that the magistrate render exact recompense, the restraint of excessive vengeance, the penalty bounded by the injury, the truth that this lex talionis was the measure of public justice, the equity of God's law, 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth,' Exodus 21:24; Leviticus 24:20, the figure — the principle of exact justice that Christ took up to forbid private retaliation, 'ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye... but I say unto you, That ye resist not evil,' Matthew 5:38-39, the truth that the measured justice of the law, rightly a rule for the magistrate, was abused as a licence for private revenge, which Christ corrected, the figure of the justice that is exact and impartial, neither pitying the guilty nor exceeding the crime, the law that bounds punishment and forbids both excess and partiality, the figure of the law of exact and measured retribution, the penalty equal to the crime); the judge not to pity but render exact justice, no partiality to the guilty (the strict justice, Leviticus 24:20) — the rule (no pity); 'but life shall go for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot' (the penalty matching the crime exactly, the measured justice given to the magistrate, taken up by Christ to forbid private revenge, the lex talionis, Exodus 21:24; Matthew 5:38-39) — the figure (the exact and impartial justice of the law, the providence, the law, the laws of war to follow, ch.20) — Keil, Calvin, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'eye for eye, tooth for tooth' establish?

sides.position: The principle of exact and measured justice - the penalty equal to the crime, a rule for the magistrate (not private revenge) that bounds punishment and forbids partiality, the equity of God's law (Exodus 21:24; Leviticus 24:20), taken up by Christ to correct the abuse that made it a licence for private retaliation (Matthew 5:38-39)

themes: And thine eye shall not pity; but life shall go for life, eye for eye, tooth for tooth, hand for hand, foot for foot
the law of just retribution, life for life and eye for eye (the crux of the principle of exact and measured justice)
the penalty equal to the injury, a rule for the magistrate not for private revenge, the equity of God's law
taken up by Christ to correct the abuse that made it a licence for retaliation (Exodus 21:24; Leviticus 24:20; Matthew 5:38-39)
